summary: The Lead Detail screen provides a reasonable information architecture across three columns — About, Activity Timeline, and Slack Channel — but suffers from significant visual noise, inconsistent iconography, and several accessibility violations that impede efficient use. The activity timeline toolbar presents too many unlabelled icon-only action buttons, increasing cognitive load and excluding keyboard and screen-reader users. Color contrast issues in secondary text and link elements, combined with absent visible focus indicators, reduce the screen's WCAG 2.2 compliance meaningfully.

issues.title: Icon-only action buttons in activity toolbar have no accessible labels
issues.location: Activity timeline toolbar — four grouped icon buttons (email, calendar, phone, list) with dropdown chevrons
issues.problem: The four primary action buttons in the activity bar (email, event, call, task) are rendered as icons only, with no visible text label or discernible accessible name visible. Screen-reader users cannot determine the purpose of each button, and sighted users must rely on prior knowledge of Salesforce conventions to interpret them. This creates a complete gulf of execution for new or assistive-technology users.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 1.1.1 (Non-text Content, Level A) requires that all non-text UI controls have a text alternative. Additionally, Norman's Gulf of Execution principle states that users cannot act when the interface fails to communicate available actions clearly.
issues.recommendation: Add visible text labels beneath or beside each icon button (e.g. 'Email', 'Event', 'Call', 'Task') and ensure each button has an aria-label attribute matching the visible label. Use the split-button pattern only after the primary action is clearly labelled.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.1.1 Non-text Content (A): all non-text controls must have a text alternative; The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Gulf of Execution: user cannot figure out how to do what they want

issues.title: Low-contrast secondary and metadata text throughout activity timeline
issues.location: Activity timeline — event subtitles ('You have an upcoming event', 'You logged a call', 'You had a task') and date/time stamps
issues.problem: The secondary descriptive text beneath each activity item and the timestamp values appear in a light grey (~#8A8A8A on white), which fails the WCAG 4.5:1 minimum contrast ratio for normal-sized text. Users with low vision or situational impairments (bright ambient light) will struggle to read these details. This directly undermines the core purpose of the timeline — communicating what happened and when.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 1.4.3 (Contrast Minimum, AA) requires a 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text. Reduced contrast also conflicts with the Designing User Interfaces (Malewicz) principle that hierarchy must be established through contrast, not just size alone.
issues.recommendation: Increase secondary text color to at least #767676 on white (4.5:1) or preferably #595959 for comfortable reading. Audit all grey text tokens across the component system and replace sub-threshold values with accessible equivalents.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum (AA): normal text requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio; Designing User Interfaces (Malewicz) — Contrast and visual hierarchy

issues.title: No visible focus indicator present on any interactive element
issues.location: Entire screen — navigation icons, form edit pencil icons, activity buttons, links
issues.problem: There is no evidence of a visible keyboard focus ring on any interactive element in the screenshot. Keyboard-only users and switch-device users cannot determine which element is currently focused, making the entire page effectively unusable without a pointing device. This is a critical blocker for users who rely on sequential keyboard navigation.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 2.4.7 (Focus Visible, AA) mandates that any keyboard-operable interface shows a visible focus indicator. Removing focus styles is one of the most common and most impactful accessibility regressions in enterprise SaaS products.
issues.recommendation: Restore the browser default focus outline or implement a custom 2px solid focus ring with at least 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors on all interactive elements. Never suppress :focus-visible styles globally in the stylesheet.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 2.4.7 Focus Visible (AA): keyboard focus indicator must be visible

issues.title: Hick's Law violation: excessive simultaneous choices in the toolbar with no hierarchy
issues.location: Activity timeline toolbar — four split-button action groups rendered at equal visual weight
issues.problem: Four equal-weight split-button controls are presented simultaneously with no primary action distinction, forcing users to evaluate all options before acting. Combined with unlabelled icons, the decision time is significantly increased. This is especially damaging for new users who have not memorized the icon vocabulary.
issues.critical_reason: Hick's Law (Psychology of Design) states that decision time increases logarithmically with the number of choices. Presenting four visually identical action groups with no hierarchy multiplies cognitive load unnecessarily, slowing task completion and increasing error rates.
issues.recommendation: Identify the single most common action (likely 'Log a Call' or 'New Email') and elevate it as a primary CTA button with a text label; demote secondary actions into a single 'More' dropdown. This reduces the initial choice set from 8+ options to 2.
issues.reference: Psychology of Design — Hick's Law: too many choices slows decisions; Universal Principles of Design — Progressive Disclosure: show only what is needed at each step

issues.title: Truncated email subject line loses critical context without recovery path
issues.location: Activity timeline — email item 'Introduction and potential collaboration - SLMob...'
issues.problem: The email subject line is truncated mid-word with an ellipsis ('SLMob...') and there is no tooltip, expand control, or hover affordance that clearly reveals the full text. Users cannot determine the full subject without clicking into the record, adding an unnecessary navigation step. This is especially problematic in a sales context where subject line recognition drives action decisions.
issues.critical_reason: Don't Make Me Think (Krug) — users should never have to wonder about truncated content; forcing a click to reveal obvious information breaks the scanning pattern and violates the principle that interface elements should minimize required effort to extract meaning.
issues.recommendation: Show the full subject line on hover via a native title attribute tooltip or a custom tooltip component. Alternatively, widen the timeline column or use a two-line text block to display the full subject without truncation.
issues.reference: Don't Make Me Think (Krug) — Billboard Design 101: users scan, not read; Universal Principles of Design — Signal-to-Noise Ratio: maximise relevant information, minimise clutter

issues.title: Trial expiry banner lacks urgency hierarchy and action proximity
issues.location: Top navigation bar — 'Days left in Starter trial: 27' with 'Buy Now' button
issues.problem: The trial countdown banner uses a muted dark-navy background that blends into the top bar, reducing the perceived urgency of a business-critical conversion prompt. The '27 days' figure and 'Buy Now' CTA are visually subordinate to the Salesforce logo and search bar. Users at risk of service interruption may not register the urgency until it is too late.
issues.critical_reason: Loss aversion (Kahneman, referenced in Psychology of Design) is the strongest motivator in trial conversion — users are more motivated by the prospect of losing access than by gaining features. Suppressing visual urgency undermines this psychological lever and reduces conversion.
issues.recommendation: Use a high-contrast warning color (amber or red) for the trial bar background as days decrease below a threshold (e.g. <7 days). Make the 'Buy Now' button a filled primary style and increase font weight on the countdown number to draw the eye.
issues.reference: Psychology of Design — Loss Aversion: people are more motivated by potential loss than equivalent gain; Refactoring UI — Use color to support hierarchy, not communicate it alone

issues.title: Edit affordance uses low-visibility pencil icons with undersized touch targets
issues.location: About panel — pencil/edit icons at the right edge of each field row (Name, Company, Title, etc.)
issues.problem: Each field in the About section has a small pencil icon as the sole edit affordance, appearing only on hover (implied by their static low-opacity rendering). The touch target appears to be approximately 16–20px, well below the WCAG 2.2 recommended 24×24px minimum and far below the 44×44px target for mobile comfort. Mobile or motor-impaired users will struggle to reliably activate these controls.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 2.5.8 (Target Size Minimum, AA) requires interactive targets to be at least 24×24 CSS pixels. Fitts' Law (Universal Principles of Design) further establishes that smaller, edge-positioned targets require disproportionately more time and precision to activate.
issues.recommendation: Increase the clickable area of each edit icon to a minimum 44×44px transparent hit area using padding. Consider switching to an inline-edit pattern where clicking the field value itself activates editing, removing the need for a separate small icon entirely.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 2.5.8 Target Size Minimum (AA): targets must be at least 24×24 CSS pixels; Universal Principles of Design — Fitts' Law: larger and closer targets are faster to click

issues.title: Slack Channel panel competes visually with primary lead data at equal weight
issues.location: Right-hand Slack Channel panel — full-height promotional illustration and text block
issues.problem: The Slack Channel panel occupies the same visual tier as the core lead data (About) and the activity timeline, despite being a secondary integration upsell. The large illustration and bold 'Better collaboration with Slack' heading draw the eye away from actionable lead information. This creates a false equivalence in the information hierarchy that can slow primary task completion.
issues.critical_reason: Gestalt Law of Figure-Ground (Psych 101, Kleinman) requires that primary content be visually distinguished from background or supplementary elements. When secondary promotional content matches primary content in visual weight, users must expend extra effort to re-establish task focus.
issues.recommendation: Reduce the Slack panel to a collapsed or subdued state by default (smaller heading, no illustration, subtle background tint). Allow users to expand it on demand, following the Progressive Disclosure principle so primary sales tasks are not visually contested.
issues.reference: Psych 101 (Kleinman) — Gestalt: Law of Figure-Ground; Universal Principles of Design — Progressive Disclosure: show only what is needed at each step

issues.title: Unsaved changes indicator ('*') on tab lacks explanatory text for all users
issues.location: Tab bar — '* Jane Doe | Lead' tab with asterisk prefix
issues.problem: The asterisk before 'Jane Doe | Lead' is the only signal that the record has unsaved changes, relying entirely on a visual convention that is not universally understood and provides no accessible text alternative. Screen-reader users will hear the tab announced as 'asterisk Jane Doe Lead' with no explanation of its meaning. New users may also not recognize the asterisk as an unsaved state indicator.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 1.3.3 (Sensory Characteristics, A) requires that instructions not rely solely on sensory characteristics such as shape or symbol. Relying on a single character symbol to communicate state also violates Norman's Feedback principle — the system must clearly confirm the consequence of user actions.
issues.recommendation: Replace or supplement the asterisk with an aria-label on the tab reading 'Jane Doe, Lead – unsaved changes'. Add a visible tooltip on hover with the text 'You have unsaved changes' and consider adding a subtle colored dot indicator alongside the asterisk for sighted users.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.3.3 Sensory Characteristics (A): instructions must not rely solely on sensory or symbolic cues; The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Feedback: every action must produce an immediate, clear response
